Short Description (Tooltip): The government's core fiscal capacity to absorb shocks without compromising routine spending.
A domestically allocated government contingency reserve, distinct from the PAF reserve, held for routine fiscal volatility management.
Pre-arranged finance held ready for rapid release when a shock trigger is met.
The primary stream of government income entering the fiscal system.
The standard budget execution pipeline from approval through to community-level service delivery.
Finance released through pre-arranged mechanisms when a shock trigger is activated.
Emergency finance flows that exist but whose activation, timing, and routing are not pre-defined.
Long-term climate investment flows entering the fiscal system, vulnerable to displacement by crisis spending.
Legislative or executive approval required before funds can be released into the routine expenditure pipeline.
Treasury release of appropriated funds to spending units — the control gate.
Formal commitment of released funds to a specific expenditure — the compliance gate.
Actual payment to supplier, service provider, or beneficiary — the oversight gate.
A two-way valve governing the allocation of funds from the fiscal reservoir into the domestic contingency reserve, and their return when needed.
An automatic or semi-automatic valve that opens when a pre-defined shock threshold is reached.
External emergency budget support flowing into the fiscal reservoir from international sources.
The gate governing commitment of emergency finance to specific response activities.
The point where activated emergency finance splits between on-budget sovereign channels and off-budget flows routed outside government systems.
The point where emergency finance reaches its end destinations — relief, early recovery, and SRSP.
The terminal point of the routine budget pipeline delivering core services, capital spend, and administration.
The ultimate recipient of both routine and emergency fiscal flows — where system performance is tested.
External climate finance entering the fiscal system from international climate funds and MDBs.
Non-repayable external grants entering the fiscal reservoir.
Sovereign borrowing entering the fiscal reservoir — with the debt-reduces-capacity feedback.
Post-shock budget support from MDBs and bilaterals that enters the fiscal system during crisis.
Pre-arranged financing instruments that feed the PAF reserve — parametric insurance, CAT bonds, contingency credit.
Standard humanitarian aid flowing off-budget through UN agencies, NGOs, and the Red Cross — appeal-based, outside sovereign systems, and not pre-arranged.
Accumulated debt reduces the effective shock absorption capacity of the fiscal reservoir.
Crisis events reduce government revenue collection, creating a feedback that further depletes the fiscal reservoir.
Most disaster response is implemented locally but most emergency finance is held at sovereign level — exposing communities during the critical relief phase.
